did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Deep Learning (Convolutional Neural Networks) and Machine Learning methods to classify skin lesions. Specifically, it employed pre-trained CNN architectures (EfficientNetB1, ResNet101, ConvNeXtBase, DenseNet201, NasNetLarge) and traditional ML models (KNN, SVM, Logistic Regression, Random Forest, Naive Bayes) on the ISIC-2016 and ISIC-2019 datasets to distinguish between benign and malignant skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from applying Deep Learning (CNNs like EfficientNetB1, ResNet101) and Machine Learning (KNN, SVM) models to the ISIC-2016 and ISIC-2019 datasets for skin lesion classification. It reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 83% test accuracy on ISIC-2019, 80% on ISIC-2016) and includes sections on Materials and Methods, Experimental Results, and Conclusion, indicating it is an original research study published in a conference proceedings (2024 Innovations in Intelligent Systems and Applications Conference).

title: Classification of Skin Lesions Using Deep Learning and Machine Learning Methods
journal: 2024 Innovations in Intelligent Systems and Applications Conference (ASYU)
brief_summary: This study evaluated the efficacy of Deep Learning and Machine Learning methods for classifying skin lesions into benign and malignant categories. The researchers utilized pre-trained Convolutional Neural Network architectures, including EfficientNetB1, ResNet101, ConvNeXtBase, DenseNet201, and NasNetLarge, alongside traditional ML models like KNN and SVM. Experiments were conducted on the two-class ISIC-2016 dataset and the eight-class ISIC-2019 dataset, with images resized to 224x224 pixels. The Deep Learning models achieved the highest performance, with ResNet101 reaching 83% test accuracy on ISIC-2016 and EfficientNet variants performing best on ISIC-2019. In contrast, the best Machine Learning result was 80% accuracy using the KNN model on the ISIC-2016 dataset. The findings demonstrate that Deep Learning methods outperform traditional Machine Learning approaches for this dermatological classification task.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as a target class in the ISIC-2016 dataset (1030 images). In the ISIC-2019 dataset, this category comprises 7 of the 8 classes: AK, BCC, BKL, DF, NV, SCC, and VASC.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant in ISIC-2016; 8 specific lesion classes in ISIC-2019)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification (benign vs. malignant) on the ISIC-2016 dataset and multi-class classification (8 classes: AK, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, SCC, VASC) on the ISIC-2019 dataset. Models used include CNNs (EfficientNetB1, ResNet101, etc.) and traditional ML (KNN, SVM, etc.). Performance is measured by accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2016
datasets_used.description: A two-class skin lesion dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) containing dermoscopy images. It was used to evaluate binary classification (benign vs. malignant) performance.

use.training: Used for training Deep Learning models (EfficientNetB1, ResNet101, etc.) and Machine Learning models (KNN, SVM, etc.). 70% of the data was used for training.
use.testing: Used for testing. 30% of the data (379 images) was held out as the test set.
use.other: Used for comparison with ISIC-2019 results.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: An eight-class skin lesion dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) containing dermoscopy images. It was used to evaluate multi-class classification performance.

use.training: Used for training Deep Learning models (EfficientNetB1, ResNet101, etc.) and Machine Learning models (KNN, SVM, etc.). 70% of the data was used for training.
use.testing: Used for testing. 30% of the data (7595 images) was held out as the test set.
use.other: Used for comparison with ISIC-2016 results.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, loss, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics without statistical significance testing.

ISIC-2019.description: Eight-class skin lesion classification dataset (AK, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, SCC, VASC). Images resized to 224x224. 70% training, 30% testing.

deep_learning_results.description: Performance metrics for pre-trained CNN architectures. Optimizer: Adam, Epochs: 30, Batch Size: 32, Dropout: 0.5.

machine_learning_results.description: Performance metrics for traditional ML models on pixel-coded and normalized data. 20% of training data used for validation/tuning.

ISIC-2019.notes: EfficientNetB1 achieved the highest test accuracy (76%) among deep learning models. SVM achieved the highest accuracy (60.87%) among machine learning models. Performance decreased as the number of classes increased compared to the binary classification task.

ISIC-2016.description: Two-class skin lesion classification dataset (Benign vs. Malignant). Images resized to 224x224. 70% training, 30% testing.

deep_learning_results.description: Performance metrics for pre-trained CNN architectures. Optimizer: Adam, Epochs: 30, Batch Size: 32, Dropout: 0.5.

machine_learning_results.description: Performance metrics for traditional ML models on pixel-coded and normalized data. 20% of training data used for validation/tuning. KNN optimal k=15. SVM parameters 'poly' and 'rbf' were best. Random Forest trees=2.

ISIC-2016.notes: ResNet101 achieved the highest test accuracy (83%) among deep learning models. KNN achieved the highest accuracy (80%) among machine learning models. Binary classification yielded higher success rates than the eight-class task.

architecture_used.main_model: Pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): EfficientNetB1, ResNet101, ConvNeXtBase, DenseNet201, NasNetLarge; Traditional Machine Learning: KNN, SVM, Logistic Regression, Random Forest, Naive Bayes
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models used for transfer learning (EfficientNetB1, ResNet101, ConvNeXtBase, DenseNet201, NasNetLarge)

training_details.loss_function: Categorical Crossentropy (implied by softmax output and multi-class context, though not explicitly named, 'loss' metric was tracked)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: All images resized to 224x224 pixels to standardize input dimensions for the models.
Color Format Conversion: Images converted to RGB format.
Normalization: Pixel values normalized to the [0, 1] range using minimum and maximum value calculation.
Pixel Coding: For machine learning models, image data was pixel-coded and mapped to numerical values.
Data Shuffling: The dataset was shuffled before training to prevent overfitting and ensure data diversity.
Data Splitting: 70% of the data used for training and 30% for testing; additionally, 20% of the training data was used for validation/tuning in machine learning experiments.
conclusions: Deep Learning models, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), outperform traditional Machine Learning methods in classifying skin lesions.
The EfficientNetB1 model achieved the highest test accuracy (76%) among the deep learning models on the eight-class ISIC-2019 dataset.
The ResNet101 model achieved the highest test accuracy (83%) among the deep learning models on the two-class ISIC-2016 dataset.
The K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) model achieved the highest accuracy (80%) among the traditional machine learning models on the ISIC-2016 dataset.
Binary classification tasks (ISIC-2016) yielded higher success rates and better performance compared to multi-class classification tasks (ISIC-2019).
Classification accuracy for machine learning models decreased as the number of classes increased from two to eight.
The proposed deep learning and machine learning models have the potential to assist specialists in diagnosis and treatment through real-world applications.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the ISIC-2016 and ISIC-2019 datasets, which limits the generalizability of the findings to other populations or imaging devices.
No formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to compare the performance differences between Deep Learning and Machine Learning models.
The paper does not report data leakage risks or detailed cross-validation strategies, using a simple 70/30 train-test split which may not fully prevent overfitting or bias.

Performance on the multi-class ISIC-2019 dataset (max 76% accuracy) was notably lower than on the binary ISIC-2016 dataset (max 83% accuracy), suggesting potential difficulty in distinguishing fine-grained classes.
The study is a conference paper with limited scope, lacking ablation studies or hyperparameter optimization details beyond basic grid search for ML models.

Clinical relevance is claimed but not empirically validated; the study does not assess diagnostic utility, false positive/negative rates in a clinical context, or comparison with dermatologist performance.
